planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial to full shade; seeds require cold stratification and germinate in early spring — bulb offsets may be planted in fall for faster establishment.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly alkaline to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade, or plant bulb offsets 2–3 inches deep in fall; ramsons naturalizes readily by seed and offsets to form dense colonies under deciduous trees, though the foliage dies back completely by early summer and must be located carefully to avoid disturbing the dormant bulbs. Allium ursinum is a European native wild garlic with broad, glossy, bright-green, strap-shaped leaves (resembling lily of the valley) that carpet the woodland floor in spring and bear strong garlic flavor and aroma; the leaves, bulbs, and the attractive white, star-shaped umbel flowers in late spring are all edible and highly prized in European culinary traditions — the fresh leaves can be used exactly like garlic in pestos, soups, salads, and compound butters. It naturalizes to form spectacular carpets under deciduous trees and is one of the finest and most useful edible groundcovers for partial to full shade; plant away from lily of the valley (Convallaria) which is toxic and has similar foliage.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs in fall 2–3 inches deep in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; meadow garlic naturalizes readily and can form dense colonies in moist meadows and open woodland.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; meadow garlic naturalizes readily by bulbils (produced instead of seeds in many populations) and will colonize moist meadows and woodland edges over time. Allium canadense is a native North American wild onion found across the eastern and central US in moist meadows, open woodlands, and disturbed areas; it produces slender, hollow, grass-like leaves and 12–24-inch stems bearing rounded umbels that typically produce bulbils (small bulbs) rather than flowers or a mixture of both in spring. All parts are edible and have a mild onion-garlic flavor; the leaves, young bulbs, and bulbils were widely used by Native Americans and are excellent as a wild substitute for chives or green onions in cooking. It is extremely adaptable and tolerates light shade, wet soils, and neglect, making it one of the easiest native edibles to establish.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2 inches deep in fall in well-drained, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; nodding onion tolerates poor, dry soils and is excellent in rock gardens and dry prairie plantings.

directions: Plant bulbs 2 inches deep in fall in well-drained, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; nodding onion is exceptionally adaptable and tolerates poor, dry, rocky soils where most alliums would struggle. Allium cernuum is a native wildflower of dry prairies, rocky slopes, and open woodlands across much of North America, named for its distinctive drooping, nodding flowerhead — the flower stalk curves or bends at the top so the umbel of small, pink to white, bell-shaped flowers faces downward in a charming, pendant cluster in mid-summer. All parts are edible with a mild onion flavor, and the plant was an important food source for many Native American peoples; it is also an excellent pollinator plant, attracting native bees, butterflies, and beneficial insects. Outstanding in rock gardens, dry meadows, and native prairie restorations; it self-seeds freely without being invasive.

planting.note: Plant bulbs or bulbils in fall 2 inches deep in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; crow garlic is naturalized throughout North America and spreads aggressively — site with care as it can become invasive in lawns and gardens.

directions: Plant small bulbs or bulbils 2 inches deep in fall in any well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; crow garlic is extremely adaptable and cold-hardy, naturalizing in lawns, roadsides, and disturbed ground throughout much of North America — control by harvesting the bulbils before they disperse if you want to limit its spread. Allium vineale is a European native now widely naturalized in North America, producing slender, hollow, cylindrical, dark-green leaves in late fall through spring and tall scapes bearing umbels of bulbils (and sometimes pinkish flowers) in early summer; it is the "wild garlic" most commonly encountered as a lawn weed in the eastern US. All parts are strongly garlic-flavored and fully edible; the leaves can be harvested in fall through spring and used like garlic chives, and the small bulbs are edible as a substitute for garlic. It is best harvested as a forage plant rather than deliberately cultivated due to its weedy nature.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in partial to full shade; wood sorrel is a classic woodland groundcover of cool, moist, conifer and hardwood forest floors.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, strongly to moderately acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5) in partial to full shade; wood sorrel requires cool, consistently moist conditions and performs poorly in heat and drought, making it a plant for zones 3–7 and cool, moist microclimates. Oxalis acetosella is a delicate, low-growing European and Asian native found in cool, moist woodland floors; it has characteristic shamrock-like, trifoliate, bright-green leaves that fold down at night and in bright light, and bears small, white flowers with pink veins in spring. The leaves have a distinctive, sharp, lemony-sour taste from oxalic acid and are used as a garnish, in salads, and to make refreshing cold drinks in Scandinavian and European folk traditions; the flavor is best when the plant is growing in cool shade. Consume in moderation as oxalic acid can be problematic in large quantities, especially for those prone to kidney stones.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 1–2 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; violet wood sorrel is one of the most charming native spring wildflowers of eastern open woodlands.

directions: Plant small bulbs 1–2 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; violet wood sorrel tolerates drier soil and more sun than O. acetosella and is better suited to most eastern US garden conditions, naturalizing in open woodlands, dry meadows, and thin lawns. Oxalis violacea is a native eastern North American spring wildflower producing small clusters of violet to rose-pink, 5-petaled flowers with a yellow throat above clover-like, trifoliate, green to purple-tinged leaves in mid-spring, and often reblooming again in fall; the bulbs multiply slowly to form colonies in well-drained soils. The leaves and flowers are edible with the characteristic sour, lemony oxalis flavor and can be used as a garnish or in salads; consume in moderation due to oxalic acid content. It is a charming, low-growing native for rock gardens, shaded borders, and naturalizing under deciduous trees.
